---
title: "31 · Deep Q-Network: Target Networks and Replay Buffers"
subtitle: "DQN tames the deadly triad with two engineering tricks: experience replay and a target network."
lesson-id: "mfrl.deep-q-network"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.q-learning-with-fa"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "game-dev"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest:

1. Can you state the [Q-learning](/glossary.qmd#q-learning)-with-FA update
   from [Lesson 30](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd)?
2. Can you name the three ingredients of the **deadly triad**?
3. Do you know what a *neural network* is, well enough to picture one as a
   parameterised function $f(x; w)$?
4. Are you comfortable with mini-batch SGD on a squared loss?

If not, revisit the previous lesson and the gradient lessons.
:::

## Touch

Imagine the screen of an Atari game — *Breakout*, say — at 84×84 grayscale,
four frames stacked. That's $84 \times 84 \times 4 = 28{,}224$ raw pixel
inputs. The state space — every possible such image — has more than $10^{67}$
elements. There is no table that fits.

Now imagine a small convolutional network that takes those four frames in and
emits 18 numbers, one per joystick action: $\hat q(s, a; w)$. Click "play
one episode" and the agent uses these Q-values to pick moves. Click "train
one batch" and a chunk of past transitions, sampled randomly from a
**replay buffer**, is fed into the network to update $w$.

The whole thing is Q-learning with neural-network FA. Lesson 30 told us
that's the deadly triad — divergence is on the menu. And yet this very setup,
in 2013, learnt to play seven Atari games at human level, from raw pixels.

::: {.lesson-beat}
The math says it should diverge. The engineering makes it not.
:::

## Wonder

1. The replay buffer holds millions of past transitions and we sample
   randomly. Why would *not* using the most recent transition possibly help?
2. The "target network" is a frozen copy of the Q-network, updated only every
   few thousand steps. What problem could a *frozen* copy possibly solve?
3. Tabular Q-learning was provably stable. We've added neural networks and
   off-policy data — every triad ingredient. So why does any of this work?

## Discover

Work in order.

### Problem 1 — write the loss

We want to do gradient steps on a Q-network. Pretend, for a moment, that the
target $r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat q(s', a'; w)$ is a fixed scalar $y$. Write
the squared-error loss for a transition $(s, a, r, s')$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Standard regression loss between the prediction $\hat q(s, a; w)$ and the
target $y$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$L(w) \;=\; \tfrac12 \bigl(y - \hat q(s, a; w)\bigr)^2,
\qquad y \;=\; r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat q(s', a'; w).$$

If $y$ were genuinely fixed, $\nabla_w L = -(y - \hat q(s,a;w)) \nabla_w \hat q(s, a; w)$
— this is plain SGD. The trouble is $y$ depends on the *same* $w$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — freeze the target

Suppose we keep a *second* copy of the network, $\hat q(\cdot; w^-)$, and let
the target use it: $y = r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat q(s', a'; w^-)$. We
**don't** update $w^-$ each step — we update $w$ each step, then once every
$C$ steps we copy $w$ into $w^-$.

What does this buy us?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Now $y$ doesn't move during the $C$ steps. The loss has a fixed target.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For the duration of $C$ steps, the regression problem
$L(w) = \tfrac12(y - \hat q(s, a; w))^2$ has a *fixed* target $y$. SGD on a
fixed-target regression is well-understood — no semi-gradient pathology, no
chasing-your-tail dynamics. We get $C$ honest gradient steps before the
target moves.

This is the **target network**. The "deadly" part of bootstrapping was that
the target shifted under us; freezing it for $C$ steps damps that.
</details>

### Problem 3 — break sample correlation

In an episode, consecutive transitions are highly correlated: if you're in
the upper-left of the screen, you're likely still there a frame later.
Standard SGD assumes IID samples. Heavily correlated mini-batches make the
gradient noisy in a structured way that biases learning.

What if we kept a buffer of the last $N$ transitions and sampled mini-batches
*uniformly at random* from it?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sampling from a large pool of past transitions roughly de-correlates them.
Each sample is from a different episode, time-step, and behaviour-policy
era.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
This is the **replay buffer** (Lin 1992; Mnih et al. 2013). Three benefits:

1. **De-correlation** — consecutive batches don't share strong temporal
   structure.
2. **Data efficiency** — each transition is replayed many times, not used
   once and discarded.
3. **Smoothing the data distribution** — the effective $\mu$ is an average
   over many recent behaviour policies, which is closer to stationary
   than any single policy's distribution.
</details>

### Problem 4 — what about the deadly triad?

DQN still has all three triad ingredients: bootstrapping, off-policy,
neural-network FA. Have we *fixed* the math?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Has Tsitsiklis–Van Roy 1997 been extended to non-linear FA? Has anyone
proved DQN converges?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No.** There is still no general convergence proof for DQN. Replay buffers
and target networks are *engineering stabilisers* — they make the bad
behaviour rare in practice, but they don't break the triad. DQN can still
diverge or oscillate, and it does in some workloads. The honest framing:
*we found two tricks that make Q-learning + neural network FA empirically
trainable on most tasks anyone has tried, even though the underlying
algorithm is not theoretically sound.*
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**DeepMind, December 2013, NeurIPS Deep Learning workshop:** Mnih et al.'s
"Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning" introduced DQN, achieving
human-level scores on seven Atari 2600 games **from raw pixels**. The
follow-up *Nature* paper in 2015 extended to 49 games, and is widely cited
as the moment modern deep RL began. Two engineering tricks — replay
buffer and target network — and the field broke open.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**DQN is Q-learning with a replay buffer (decorrelates samples) and a
target network (stabilises the bootstrap target).** It does not break the
deadly triad — it engineers around it. Convergence is not guaranteed; what
is guaranteed is that the algorithm trains stably on most workloads
practitioners care about.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Deep Q-Network (DQN).** A neural-network function approximator
$\hat q(s, a; w)$ trained by minimising

$$L(w) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{(s, a, r, s') \sim \mathcal D}\!\left[\bigl(r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat q(s', a'; w^-) - \hat q(s, a; w)\bigr)^2\right]$$

where:

- $\mathcal D$ is a **replay buffer** of past transitions.
- $w^-$ is a **target network**: a frozen copy of $w$, updated $w^- \leftarrow w$
  every $C$ steps.

The behaviour policy is $\varepsilon$-greedy on $\hat q(\cdot; w)$ for
exploration.

:::

## Apply

### Worked example — DQN training loop

A single iteration of DQN, in pseudo-code:

1. **Act.** From state $s_t$, choose $a_t = \arg\max_a \hat q(s_t, a; w)$
   with probability $1-\varepsilon$, else uniform random.
2. **Observe.** Receive reward $r_t$ and next state $s_{t+1}$. Append the
   tuple $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})$ to the replay buffer $\mathcal D$.
3. **Sample.** Draw a mini-batch of $B$ transitions
   $\{(s_i, a_i, r_i, s'_i)\}_{i=1}^B$ uniformly from $\mathcal D$.
4. **Build targets.** For each $i$,
   $y_i \;=\; r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat q(s'_i, a'; w^-)$.
5. **Update.**
   $w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla_w \sum_{i=1}^B \tfrac12 (y_i - \hat q(s_i, a_i; w))^2$.
6. **Sync.** Every $C$ steps, set $w^- \leftarrow w$.

Two engineering tricks. Otherwise it is just Q-learning with a neural net.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** In the loss above, are we differentiating $w^-$? Why or why not?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No.** $w^-$ is treated as a constant (it's the frozen target network).
That is exactly what makes the inner regression a well-posed
fixed-target SGD problem during the $C$ steps between syncs. (In code, this
is usually `target_q = target_net(s_next).max(dim=1).detach()`.)
</details>

**W2.** Why is replay sampling uniform rather than always the most recent
transition?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The most recent transition is highly correlated with the previous batch.
Uniform sampling from a pool of $N$ past transitions de-correlates the
data and gives more effectively-IID gradients. (Prioritised replay, a 2016
refinement, samples non-uniformly by TD-error magnitude — but uniform is
the original DQN choice and the right place to start.)
</details>

**W3.** State the relationship between $\hat q$ and $\hat q(\cdot; w^-)$
right after a target-network sync.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
They are identical: $w^- = w$. Over the next $C$ updates $w$ drifts, while
$w^-$ stays fixed; then they are forced equal again at the next sync.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** What goes wrong if you set $C = 1$ (sync the target network every
step)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$C = 1$ means $w^- = w$ at all times. The target network has zero effect.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The target $y_i = r_i + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat q(s'_i, a'; w)$ now depends
on the *current* $w$. You're back to the unstabilised semi-gradient update
that caused divergence in Lesson 30. The whole point of the target network
is the *delay*; eliminating the delay eliminates the trick.
</details>

**P2.** What goes wrong if you set $C$ very large (sync every million steps)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You learn fast against an outdated target.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Slow, biased learning. Between syncs your target reflects the network as it
was a million steps ago — wildly out of date. You need the target to refresh
fast enough that improvements in $\hat q$ feed back into the target. Typical
choices: $C$ between $10^3$ and $10^4$ for Atari.
</details>

**P3.** Is DQN with a tiny replay buffer (say $N = 1$, the most recent
transition) different from vanilla Q-learning with FA?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Run the DQN loop with $N = 1$ in your head.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
With $N = 1$, every "mini-batch" is just the single most recent transition,
exactly like vanilla Q-learning with FA. You lose the de-correlation
benefit. So yes — empirically, very small replay buffers behave like the
unstabilised version, and you risk divergence (Lesson 30).
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Soft target updates** are an alternative: instead of copying $w$
into $w^-$ every $C$ steps, do
$w^- \leftarrow \tau w + (1 - \tau) w^-$ at *every* step with $\tau \ll 1$
(say $\tau = 0.005$). Argue why this is morally equivalent to the hard
sync.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute the effective time scale on which $w^-$ tracks $w$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Soft updates make $w^-$ an exponential moving average of $w$ with effective
time-constant $\sim 1/\tau$. Choosing $\tau = 1/C$ gives the same average
lag as a hard sync every $C$ steps. The tracking is smoother (no
discontinuous jumps) but the stabilising mechanism is identical: the target
$y_i$ uses a slowly-moving copy of $w$ that does not chase the current
parameters. DDPG (Lesson 38) prefers soft updates; DQN as published uses
the hard sync.
</details>

**S2.** Skim the DQN architecture: 84×84×4 input, two convolutional layers,
one fully-connected layer, output head with 18 (Atari) action-values.
Approximately how many parameters does this network have? (Order of
magnitude is enough.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The fully-connected layer dominates. Estimate its size first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The 2015 DQN architecture is ~1.7 million parameters, of which the
final fully-connected layer (3136 → 512) dominates. The replay buffer
holds ~1 million transitions. Memory cost: comfortably under 1 GB.
Training to human level on a single Atari game takes ~10 million frames,
which is ~50 hours of game-play.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"DQN solved RL."** No — DQN is one *value-based* algorithm; it works
   well for discrete-action tasks with rich observations and *enough* data,
   but it is brittle, sample-inefficient, and has no convergence guarantee.
   Continuous-action problems and many real-robot tasks need the
   actor-critic family covered in Lessons 35–38.
2. **"The target network is the same as a frozen network."** Half-right.
   The target network *is* frozen for $C$ steps — but then it gets refreshed
   from the current $w$. The point is the *delay*, not permanent freezing.
3. **"Replay buffers are off-policy correction."** No — they don't reweight
   anything. They are a *de-correlation* trick. True off-policy correction
   uses [importance sampling](/glossary.qmd) (Lesson 37), which DQN
   ignores. This is fine because Q-learning's $\max_{a'}$ target doesn't
   depend on the behaviour policy at all.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **DQN = Q-learning + replay buffer + target network.** The first two
   come straight from Lesson 30; the latter two are the engineering layer
   that makes neural-net Q-learning empirically trainable.
2. **The target network turns the bootstrap target into a fixed regression
   target** for $C$ steps. This is the deepest trick: it converts a
   chasing-your-tail problem into a sequence of well-posed regressions.
3. **The replay buffer de-correlates samples** and re-uses each transition
   many times. Samples are off-policy in the strictest sense (the
   behaviour policy that generated them is no longer current), but
   Q-learning's $\max_{a'}$ target is invariant to behaviour, so we don't
   need importance correction.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you write the DQN loss from memory, including the role of $w^-$?
2. Could you explain to a friend why $C = 1$ defeats the purpose of the
   target network?
3. Do you understand that DQN does not *prove* the deadly triad away — it
   *engineers around* it?

- **3 yes:** continue to [Lesson 32 — Policy Parameterisation](../32-policy-parameterization/index.qmd).
- **2 yes:** redo P1 and P2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover*, especially Problem 4.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[mfrl.q-learning-with-fa](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd)** — the
  deadly triad and the divergence DQN must defeat.

### What this unlocks

- **[Policy Parameterisation](../32-policy-parameterization/index.qmd)** —
  swap value-based learning for direct policy optimisation, which DQN
  cannot do for continuous actions.
- The actor-critic family (Lessons 35–38), which adopts DQN's stabilisers
  and adds policy gradients.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** DQN is the algorithm that opened the door to modern deep RL.
Every value-based deep agent since 2015 — Double DQN, Dueling DQN,
Distributional DQN (C51), Rainbow, IQN — extends this skeleton. Even
actor-critic methods (DDPG, TD3, SAC) borrow the replay buffer and target
network nearly verbatim.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** DQN-style agents are how studios research
super-human play in deterministic-environment games (board games, Atari,
many puzzle games) and difficulty-tuning bots for action games. The
sample efficiency is poor, but the wall-clock cost of running games at
1000× speed is low.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this
track expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §8 (DQN). Original references: Mnih et al. (2013),
  *Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning*, NeurIPS workshop;
  Mnih et al. (2015), *Human-level control through deep reinforcement
  learning*, Nature 518.
- Touchstones honoured: Better Explained "Aha!" framing (the key insight
  that DQN doesn't *break* the triad, it engineers around it); 3Blue1Brown
  visual framing (the Atari frame stack as a concrete state).
:::
